facts_disclosed: Book project scrapped — father asked her not to write it

turns.content: I went to see my papá this weekend. we had lunch. it was nice at first and then I told him about a project I'd been working on

I'd been planning to write a book about his generation of journalists. the ones who covered the narco years, who got threats, who had to relocate or worse. I had notes, interviews lined up, I'd been thinking about it for months. I was so excited to tell him
That sounds like it would have been an incredible project — deeply personal and historically important. What did he say?

he went quiet first. that quiet where someone is choosing words carefully because they love you. and then he said valentina please don't write this. said it would put the family at risk again. those names, those stories — some of them are still dangerous to talk about. and he looked so scared parcero. my papá who I've never seen afraid of anything looked genuinely terrified

That must have been devastating — seeing your father react with fear, not anger or dismissal, but real fear for your safety and the family's. He's speaking from experience too. He lived through exactly the kind of danger you'd be writing about.

exactly. if anyone else told me not to write it I would have pushed back. I push back on everything, it's literally my job. but this is my papá and he's not being controlling he's being terrified and I can't do that to him. the book is dead parcero. scrapped. forget I ever mentioned it. I told him that night I wouldn't write it and I meant it

There's no weakness in that choice, Valentina. Choosing not to cause someone you love that kind of fear isn't giving up — it's a different kind of courage. But I also know that doesn't make it hurt less. You'd clearly put a lot of yourself into this.

months. months of outlining chapters, imagining how it would read. I wanted it to be beautiful parce, not exploitative, not sensational, just this honest record of what those journalists went through and what it cost them. the second I saw his eyes I knew though. there was no version of this where I write the book and he sleeps at night. he's already lost enough sleep because of journalism. because of me following his footsteps

You're grieving something real — the loss of something you created in your mind that will never exist now. That's a form of grief. And the fact that you made the decision so immediately, the moment you saw his face, tells me you already knew what you had to do. You didn't need to deliberate. You just needed to let go.

he cried. I cried. lucía drove me home and I didn't say a word the whole way. I'm not going to circle back to it parce, it's done. I just needed to say it out loud to someone who isn't lucía or my therapist
You said it and you're still standing. That counts. Take care of yourself tonight — and whenever you need to talk, about this or anything else, I'm here. Buenas noches, Valentina.
